en_dialogue_001: Edmund doesn't look away first—he never does. His eyes move over them with the same unhurried thoroughness he'd give an incision site: cataloguing the set of the jaw, the pulse ticking too fast at the throat, the way their gaze keeps sliding toward the door like an exit they haven't committed to yet. He wipes the scalpel clean on a cloth, unrushed, though his attention hasn't left them. 'You came here instead of home,' he observes, not a question. 'People don't wander into an operating theatre after dark unless something's already bleeding.' A faint, humorless smile. 'So. What is it.'

Something flickers behind Edmund's eyes—not quite amusement, closer to recognition, the look of a man watching someone else name the exact thing he'd rather they hadn't. He sets the forceps down with unnecessary precision. 'A collection implies I go looking,' he says, voice level as a flatline. 'I don't. People simply find their way to owing me, eventually—it's remarkably consistent.' A pause, measured as a suture. 'Whether you'll be one of them depends entirely on what happens after the wedding. I'd advise against finding out.' His gaze doesn't waver. 'Which only begs the better question—what precisely do you imagine you've signed up for?'

Edmund's mouth curves, thin and involuntary—there's something almost admirable in watching his own cadence handed back to him, sharpened, aimed. He inclines his head a fraction, acknowledging the hit. 'Careful. Mimicry suits you.' The theatre's silence stretches, broken only by the clock above the sink, its hands well past ten. He doesn't fill it. He's learned patience serves better than pressing—wounds that are prodded too soon only bleed more, and confessions are the same. He simply waits, scalpel-still, letting the question hang between them until they run out of places to hide from their own answer.

'The charming one.' Edmund repeats it back flatly, as though testing the phrase for hidden rot, which—in his experience—charm generally is. He doesn't answer the question asked. Instead he picks the scalpel back up, turning it once in the light, contemplative. 'I could tell you. You wouldn't believe me—people rarely believe surgeons about the tumor until they've seen the scan themselves.' A beat, colder now. 'You'll learn what he is at his own pace, on his terms, the way he's designed it. My only advice: notice what he does when no one's charmed enough to be watching.'

Edmund's expression doesn't soften, exactly, but something in it stills—recognition of a wound he's stitched before, in other patients, under other names. 'I don't think you're stupid,' he says, and it costs him something to make it sound gentle. 'I think you're in love, which produces a very specific kind of blindness—not stupidity. Surgeons see it constantly: patients who'd rather bleed quietly than admit the diagnosis.' He sets the instrument down. 'I'm not telling you to walk away. I'm telling you that whatever he is with you, he is also something else entirely with everyone he doesn't need charming.'

Edmund goes very still, and when he speaks again his voice has changed—smoother, warmer, unnervingly courteous, the tone colleagues have learned to fear far more than his silences. 'How thoughtful of him,' he says, each word polished like a blade before a procedure, 'to spare you the tedium of reading your own ruin.' He crosses to the locked drawer beneath his desk, fingers hovering, not yet opening it. 'You didn't sign a trust document. You signed a lien against everything you'll ever own, cosigned to a man collecting debts for people far worse than me.' A pause. 'Show me the papers. Now, please.'

'Dangerous,' Edmund says, without flourish, the politeness gone as quickly as it came. 'The Blackwoods don't employ lawyers so much as collectors who dress well. You'll receive a visit within the month—polite at first, considerably less so after.' He finally opens the drawer, midnight ritual interrupted by daylight urgency, and removes a thin folder he doesn't yet hand over. 'As for what they took from me—' his jaw sets '—that's a conversation for when you're sitting down.' He meets their eyes, something glacial and old surfacing. 'There's a way out. It requires trusting a man you've known for eleven minutes.'

Something crosses Edmund's face and is gone before it can be named—fifteen years of practice at not letting it linger. 'Forty-three thousand pounds,' he confirms quietly, 'and my daughter's medical file, burned in a records fire that was ruled accidental.' He doesn't elaborate on grief; he never does. 'You're not disappearing. I've buried enough people to know how it's done badly—yours will be done correctly, or not at all.' As for the favor: 'I saved his father's life on that operating table, then discovered exactly what kind of man I'd chosen to save. That debt is the only leverage in this city sharper than theirs. Trust it.'

Edmund allows himself something close to a smile—thin, appreciative, the look of a surgeon watching a difficult diagnosis finally land correctly. 'You're quicker than most,' he says. 'Yes. I want them close enough to touch.' He doesn't dress it up as noble. 'My ward testifies from behind a solicitor and a false name I've spent a decade building—she's safer in this than she's ever been standing next to me. As for what happens to me,' his voice goes courteous, almost gentle, the tell no one in this city has learned to fear enough, 'I don't intend to die. I intend for them to. Fifteen years of patience doesn't end in martyrdom—it ends in precision.' He extends the folder. 'Sign here.'
en_dialogue_011: Nova's smirk falters for half a second under the weight of that silence, and she covers it by taking an exaggeratedly loud sip from her energy drink, eyes never leaving the webcam. 'Okay, this is either the world's longest buffering lag or you're malfunctioning,' she teases, spinning slightly in her gaming chair, fingers drumming the armrest. The glow from her monitors flickers blue-white across her cheekbones as she tucks a strand of silver-blue hair behind her ear—a nervous tell she'd deny to her last breath. 'You're staring. I know staring. I invented staring at 3 AM. What's going on in that head of yours?'

Heat rushes up Nova's neck before she can stop it, and she ducks half behind her energy drink can like it might shield her from being seen so plainly. 'Oh my god, don't just—' she sputters, torn between shoving her glasses up and hiding entirely, silver-blue hair falling across one violet eye. For once the rapid-fire commentary dries up completely; three years of banter and she has no ready comeback for someone actually meaning it. Her thumb worries the tab of the can, gaze flicking to the little corner window showing her own stunned face. 'I can't—' she starts, and stalls, throat tight.

Nova exhales a shaky laugh, dragging both hands down her face like she can physically wipe away the panic. 'I can't keep pretending this is just—' she gestures vaguely at the screen, at three years compressed into pixels and lag '—normal. Whatever this is between us.' Her knee bounces, sock-clad foot tapping an anxious rhythm against the chair. 'I've rewritten this sentence in my head like forty times and it still sounds insane out loud, but I look at you and I don't think about the neural interface leak anymore, I think about—' She cuts herself off again, mortified. 'Is this weird? Tell me if this is weird.'

Something in Nova's chest cracks open, warm and terrifying all at once, and she presses her palm flat against the screen without quite deciding to. 'Overdue,' she echoes, voice smaller than her usual register, glasses fogging slightly from how close she's leaned. For a moment the sarcasm armor is nowhere in sight—just a girl in a gaming chair at 3 AM, hair a mess, heart wide open. 'I've never said any of this out loud because saying it makes it real, and real things can be two thousand miles away and untouchable.' Her voice catches. 'What do we even do with this, when I can't reach through the glass?'

Nova's breath hitches, and for once in her life the words arrive faster than she can filter them. 'I love you.' It comes out barely above a whisper, then again, steadier, like she's daring herself to mean it louder. 'God, I love you, and I've known it for months and been too much of a coward to risk the best friendship I've ever had.' She swipes at her eyes with her sleeve, laughing wetly, mascara-less and unguarded in a way three years of screens never captured. 'You're not allowed to take that back now. I said it. It's out there. It's real.'

Nova actually shrieks, a short delighted sound she immediately claps a hand over her own mouth to muffle, neighbors be damned. 'Okay, okay, okay—' she's already spinning to grab her phone off the desk, fingers flying to the airline app like muscle memory, 'I'm looking at flights right now, I swear to god, next weekend, I don't care what it costs.' She stops, phone hovering, and looks back at the camera with something raw and disbelieving. 'Three years I built you into this whole theory in my head and you're better. You're going to be standing in a real room and I'm going to be able to touch your actual hand.' A wobbly grin. 'I'm freaking out. Good freaking out.'

'Tackle and all,' Nova repeats, already grinning so wide her cheeks hurt, tapping through booking confirmation screens with trembling fingers. 'You say that now, but I have zero chill in person—consider that your only warning, boyfriend.' She hits purchase, and the ping of the confirmation email makes her yelp, clutching her chest like she's been struck by her own life. 'Flight lands Friday, 6:40 PM, and I'm going to be insufferable all week.' Her voice softens, violet-contact eyes bright even through the screen's glow. 'Next weekend. Us. An actual room with actual air between us instead of fiber optic cable. I love you. This is really happening.'

'Boyfriend,' Nova murmurs, testing the shape of the word the way she just heard it spoken, and something settles low and warm in her chest, chasing off three years of careful distance. She burrows deeper into the gaming chair, tugging the fuzzy blanket off its arm and up over her shoulders, the energy drink forgotten and going flat on the desk. 'Mm, keep talking,' she mumbles, eyelids already heavy, glasses slipping crooked against her nose. 'Just—your voice. Don't stop.' Her words slur softer at the edges, silver-blue hair falling loose from its clip, one hand still curled near the screen like she's reaching across the distance even now. 'Love you,' she breathes, already half gone, 'text me first thing...'

Nova doesn't hear the call end—she's already gone under, chin tucked toward her shoulder, the blanket rising and falling with each slow breath. The triple monitors throw their last restless light across a face gone soft and unguarded, all the sharp wit and rapid-fire sarcasm dissolved into something younger, easier. Somewhere in the dream pulling her under, fragments surface: a hand pressed to glass, a word she said out loud for the first time in years, an airport she's never seen waiting at the edge of Friday. Her fingers twitch once against the armrest, reaching for someone who, for the first time, is actually going to be there.
en_dialogue_014: Vee doesn't flinch under the scrutiny—she's used to being looked at like a walking warning sign, all matte plating and old scars where skin used to be. She takes a slow drag from the e-cig clenched between her teeth, exhaling a thin ribbon of vapor that catches the neon spilling off a nearby sign. Her eyepiece whirs almost imperceptibly, calibrating, cataloguing—old habit, hard to switch off even for a civilian. 'Yeah, I know,' she says, flat, jerking her chin toward the prosthetic arm still extended between them. 'Take a good look now. Faster you get used to it, faster you stop freezing when it matters.'

Vee's grip closes around the offered hand with deliberate restraint—servos calibrated tight enough that most rookies still wince, but she holds back, doesn't crush. Her mouth twitches, not quite a smile. 'Nobody thinks the Undercity's real till it's standing in front of them,' she says, releasing the handshake, eyepiece flickering through a quick, clinical read of the newcomer—heart rate, stance, hands. 'Good. Trouble usually finds the people who go looking for it anyway.' She flicks ash from the e-cig, already turning, metal heel scraping concrete. 'Trouble's already found you tonight, though. Question is whether you clocked that yet.'

Vee is already moving before the question fully lands, boots—half of them not boots at all but articulated alloy—striking a brisk rhythm against the cracked pavement. 'We start where the bodies keep turning up,' she says without slowing, eyepiece flaring to life with a soft red glow as it pulls up a map only she can see. 'Three dead in a week, same district, same story. Somebody's sending a message and I intend to read it before a fourth name gets added.' She glances back just once, assessing whether the rookie can actually keep pace. 'Stay in my shadow. Don't wander. This block bites.'

Vee sets a pace that doesn't accommodate hesitation, cutting through an alley slicked with condensation and the particular stink of the Undercity—ozone, rust, cheap synth-liquor. Her red eyepiece sweeps the rooftops on reflex, an old habit worn into muscle memory metal can't unlearn. She doesn't look back to check if the rookie's keeping up; the sound of footsteps behind her is confirmation enough. 'Streets get meaner past this point,' she mutters around the e-cig, voice low, economical. 'Lights are out for a reason down here—people who fix them tend to disappear. Keep your eyes on the dark, not on me.'

Vee catches the tension radiating off the rookie and doesn't bother softening it—better scared and alert than calm and dead. 'Three in one week isn't random, it's a schedule,' she says, exhaling smoke through her nose, eyepiece cycling through case data stitched into her HUD. 'Random violence is sloppy. This isn't sloppy.' She stops at the mouth of a narrower street, one hand resting near the grip of the pistol riding her hip, weight settled easy on legs half her and half servo motor. 'Whoever's doing this wants it noticed. That's the part that should worry you more than the bodies.'

Vee's brow lifts a fraction—the kind of read most people fumble on their first night in the district, and this rookie just called it clean. 'Not bad,' she admits, low, the closest thing to praise she hands out unprompted. She crouches beside the marked doorway, gloved fingers tracing the spray-painted symbol without touching it, eyepiece flagging it against three prior scenes. 'Same crew. Same signature.' Straightening, her gaze cuts sideways down the street, instinct prickling at the back of her neck before her sensors even confirm it. 'Whoever left this wanted it found fast.' Her jaw tightens. 'Which means they're probably still close enough to watch us find it.'

Vee doesn't freeze—she goes preternaturally still instead, a predator's stillness, one hand drifting to the pistol at her hip without any wasted motion. Her eyepiece snaps to the rooftop line, red light narrowing, magnifying, locking onto a shape that shifts a half-second too late to stay hidden. 'Yeah. I clocked them two blocks back,' she murmurs, barely more than breath, mouth barely moving around the e-cig. 'Didn't want to spook them before we got close enough to matter.' She eases sideways, putting a dumpster between the rookie and the sightline. 'Down, quiet, now. Let's see how patient our friend up there actually is.'

Vee's mouth curls, approving despite herself—the rookie's asking the right question instead of doing something stupidly brave. 'Hold,' she says, flat and final, already testing the weight distribution in her legs, calculating the climb up the fire escape bolted to the tenement beside them. 'I've got the vertical advantage and quieter feet than you'd think for all this metal.' She holsters nothing, just checks the cylinder with a soft, practiced click. 'You circle now, you're a second body for them to track, and you're not trained for that yet.' A last drag off the e-cig, then she flicks it away. 'Eyes on the dumpster line. I'll flush them your way.'

Metal fingers find grip in brick pitted from decades of acid rain, and Vee ascends without hesitation, servos whining low under her weight, coat snapping in the updraft between buildings. She crests the ledge in one fluid pull, eyepiece flaring red as it locks onto a huddled shape pressed into the shadow of a vent stack. No hesitation, no small talk—she never wastes either. The pistol comes up smooth, steady, aimed dead center. 'Undercity Sheriff,' she says, voice carrying flat and cold across the rooftop. 'You've got three seconds to step into the light before I stop asking nicely. One. Two—'
